params.text:
Understanding conflict in care

Conflict happens when the needs, wishes, or expectations of two or more people clash. In adult social care it can arise between you and a person you support, their family, or colleagues. Most conflict is not physical — it often shows itself as raised voices, refusal to cooperate, complaints, or an atmosphere of tension.

The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) defines work-related violence broadly as any incident in which a person is abused, threatened, or assaulted in circumstances relating to their work. This includes verbal abuse and threats, not only physical assault.

  • Conflict is a normal part of human relationships and is not always negative.
  • Handled early and calmly, most situations can be resolved before they escalate.
  • Your goal is to reduce tension, keep everyone safe, and preserve the person's dignity.

params.question: Conflict in care settings only counts as a problem once it becomes physically violent.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Conflict covers far more than physical violence. Verbal abuse, threats, refusal, and tension are all forms of conflict, and spotting them early is essential.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Conflict includes verbal abuse, threats, and tension. Recognising it early — long before any physical risk — is how you keep situations from escalating.
